---
title: "Lesson 15: Testing with Assert"
---

## Learning goals

Use `assert` to test simple functions with known answers and edge cases.

## Main ideas

A test checks whether code gives the answer you expect.

```python
def double(number):
    return number * 2

assert double(3) == 6
assert double(10) == 20
```

If an assertion passes, Python says nothing. If it fails, Python gives an error.

Test edge cases near boundaries.

```python
def is_passing(mark):
    return mark >= 50

assert is_passing(49) == False
assert is_passing(50) == True
assert is_passing(51) == True
```

## Exercises

### Exercise 1

Write and test `double(number)`.

### Exercise 2

Write and test `is_even(number)`.

### Exercise 3

Write and test `clean_text(text)`.

### Exercise 4

Write and test `check_answer(user_answer, correct_answer)`.

## Debugging practice

Fix this deliberately broken code, then explain what was wrong.

```python
# Broken on purpose: read the error, fix one thing, and run again.
if True
print("debugging practice")
```

## Challenge

Build a small program that uses this lesson's idea together with at least two earlier ideas from the course.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Click to reveal hints

- Start with the smallest working version.
- Add one feature at a time.
- Use `print()` to inspect values.
- Test with more than one example.

:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Click to reveal one possible solution or approach

```python
def clean_text(text):
    return text.strip().lower()

assert clean_text(" Python ") == "python"
assert clean_text("HELLO") == "hello"


def check_answer(user_answer, correct_answer):
    return clean_text(user_answer) == clean_text(correct_answer)

assert check_answer("Python", "python") == True
assert check_answer(" java ", "python") == False
```

:::

## What you learned

You can now use `assert` to check whether simple functions work.

## Looking ahead

Next you will save table data with CSV files.
